Module 1: Introduction to MLOps
Production AI: An Engineering Guide
Theja T ulabandhula
January 10, 2026
University of Illinois Chicago
1

Overview
Getting Started (Prerequisites)
Foundations of ML Systems
Reproducibility and Dependencies
CI/CD and Automation
Pipelines and Artifacts
Architectural Considerations
Hands-On Demos
Lab Exercise
Assessments
Key T akeaways
2
Overview

Module Overview (cont.)
Why do 87% of ML projects never make it to production?
• This module establishes the conceptual foundations of MLOps
• Introduces the complete ML/AI lifecycle
• Covers essential software engineering practices
• Explores architectural differences between traditional ML and LLM-based systems
After completing this module, you will understand how ML systems move from
development to production.
3

What Y ou’ll Learn (cont.)
By the end of this module, you will be able to:
1. Map any ML project to the six lifecycle stages
2. Set up reproducible project environments with version control
3. Distinguish between traditional ML and LLM-based system architectures
4. Identify and prevent common sources of ML technical debt
4

Module Roadmap (cont.)
Getting Started (Prerequisites)
↓
Foundations of ML Systems
↓
Reproducibility & Dependencies
↓
CI/CD and Automation
↓
Pipelines and Artifacts
↓
Architectural Considerations
5
Getting Started (Prerequisites)

Software Setup Overview (cont.)
Software Minimum Version Purpose
Python 3.10+ Core programming language
Git 2.30+ Version control
pip 21.0+ Package management
venv (built-in) Virtual environments
pytest 7.0+ T esting framework
7

Software Setup Overview (cont.)
￿ See: Software Setup: Module 1
8

Verification Checklist (cont.)
9

Verification Checklist (cont.)
# 1. Python version
python3 --version
# Expected: Python 3.10.x or higher
# 2. Git version
git--version
# Expected: git version 2.30.x or higher
# 3. pip version
pip--version
# Expected: pip 21.x or higher
# 4. venv works
python3 -mvenv--help |head-1
# Expected: usage: venv [-h] ...
￿ See: Software Setup: Module 110

Command Line Basics (cont.)
Why CLI matters for MLOps:
• Git is primarily a command-line tool
• Virtual environments are created and activated via CLI
• CI pipelines execute shell commands
• Docker and Kubernetes are controlled via CLI
• Remote servers typically only have CLI access
￿ See: Command Line Basics for MLOps
11

Essential CLI Commands (cont.)
Command Purpose Example
pwd Print working directory pwd
ls List directory contents ls -la
cd Change directory cd ~/projects
mkdir Create directory mkdir -p path/to/dir
touch Create empty file touch file.txt
cat Display file contents cat file.txt
12

Essential CLI Commands (cont.)
￿ See: Command Line Basics for MLOps
13
Foundations of ML Systems

The ML/AI Lifecycle (cont.)
The lifecycle is circular, not linear!
￿ See: ML/AI Lifecycle Stages Overview
15

The Six Lifecycle Stages (cont.)
Stage Description Key Output
1. Data Collection Gather raw data from sources Raw datasets
2. Preprocessing Clean, transform, split data Prepared features
3. Model T raining Learn patterns from data Model artifacts
4. Evaluation Assess performance Metrics, reports
5. Deployment Serve predictions API endpoint
6. Monitoring T rack production health Alerts, dashboards
16

The Six Lifecycle Stages (cont.)
￿ See: ML/AI Lifecycle Stages Overview
17

What is Git? (cont.)
Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes to files over time.
For ML projects, Git tracks:
• Code changes to training scripts and inference services
• Configuration file updates (Y AML, JSON)
• Documentation revisions
• Dependency specifications
Git does NOT eﬀiciently track:
• Large model weights
• Datasets (use DVC or Git LFS)
￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps
18

Git’s Three States (cont.)
State Location Description
Modified Working Directory Changes made but not staged
Staged Staging Area Changes marked for next commit
Committed Repository Permanently stored snapshot
19

Git’s Three States (cont.)
￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps
20

Essential Git Commands (cont.)
21

Essential Git Commands (cont.)
# Initialize and clone
gitinit
gitclone https://github.com/username/repo.git
# Stage and commit
gitadd .
gitcommit-m"Add prediction endpoint"
# Branch and merge
gitcheckout -bfeature-branch
gitmerge feature-branch
# Push and pull
gitpush origin main
gitpull origin main
￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps22

GitHub Repository Setup (cont.)
GitHub provides:
• Remote storage for Git repositories
• Pull Requests for code review
• Issues for bug tracking
• Actions for CI/CD pipelines
• Pages for documentation hosting
Milestone 0 requires a GitHub repository as the foundation for your project.
￿ See: GitHub Repository Setup
23

Python Virtual Environments (cont.)
Avirtual environment is an isolated Python installation with its own packages.
# Create virtual environment
python-mvenv venv
# Activate (Linux/macOS)
source venv/bin/activate
# Activate (Windows)
venv\Scripts\activate
# Deactivate
deactivate
24

Python Virtual Environments (cont.)
Like containers for your project’s Python ecosystem!
￿ See: Python Virtual Environments
25

Why Virtual Environments Matter (cont.)
Without Virtual Environments With Virtual Environments
Dependency conflicts Isolation per project
“Works on my machine” Reproducible setup
Global package pollution Clean, controlled deps
Version chaos Pinned versions
26

Why Virtual Environments Matter (cont.)
￿ See: Python Virtual Environments
27

Project Organization (cont.)
mlops-project/
￿￿￿ .github/
￿ ￿￿￿ workflows/
￿ ￿￿￿ ci.yml # GitHub Actions workflow
￿￿￿ src/
￿ ￿￿￿ __init__.py # Makes src a Python package
￿￿￿ tests/
￿ ￿￿￿ test_smoke.py # Smoke tests for CI
￿￿￿ .gitignore # Files to exclude from Git
￿￿￿ requirements.txt # Pinned dependencies
￿￿￿ README.md # Project documentation
￿ See: Project Organization for MLOps
28

The .gitignore File (cont.)
29

The .gitignore File (cont.)
# Python artifacts
__pycache__/
*.py[cod]
# Virtual environments
venv/
.venv/
# Data and models (use DVC)
data/
*.pkl
*.h5
# Secrets (NEVER commit!)
.env
Create .gitignore before your first commit!
￿ See: Project Organization for MLOps30

Reproducibility and
Dependencies

What is Dependency Pinning? (cont.)
Dependency pinning = specifying exact versions of packages
# WRONG: Version ranges
numpy>=1.20
pandas>=1.3
# CORRECT: Exact versions
numpy==1.24.3
pandas==2.0.3
￿ See: Dependency Pinning & Reproducibility
32

Why Pinning Matters (cont.)
# Generate pinned requirements
pip freeze >requirements.txt
# Install from pinned file
pip install -rrequirements.txt
ML systems are sensitive to version changes:
• A minor NumPy update can alter numerical results
• A scikit-learn upgrade can change model behavior
• Same code + different versions = different results
￿ See: Dependency Pinning & Reproducibility
33

Version Specifier Options (cont.)
Specifier Meaning Use Case
==1.2.3 Exact version Production, CI
>=1.2.0 Minimum version Development only
>=1.2,<2.0 Version range Major compat
~=1.2.3 Compatible release Patch updates
34

Version Specifier Options (cont.)
Recommendation: Use== for production and CI environments.
￿ See: Dependency Pinning & Reproducibility
35

T esting Fundamentals (cont.)
T ypes of T ests:
T est T ype Purpose Speed
Unit tests T est individual functions Fast (ms)
Integration tests T est components together Medium (s)
Smoke tests Verify basic functionality Fast
End-to-end tests T est complete workflows Slow (min)
36

T esting Fundamentals (cont.)
For Milestone 0, you’ll write smoke tests .
￿ See: T esting Fundamentals for ML Projects
37

Smoke T est Example (cont.)
38

Smoke T est Example (cont.)
# tests/test_smoke.py
deftest_core_imports():
"""Verify core ML packages are importable."""
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
assertTrue
deftest_numpy_operations():
"""Verify NumPy works."""
import numpy as np
arr =np.array([ 1,2,3,4,5])
assertarr.mean() ==3.0
￿ See: T esting Fundamentals for ML Projects 39

Running T ests with pytest (cont.)
# Run all tests
pytest tests/
# Run with verbose output
pytest tests/ -v
# Run a specific test file
pytest tests/test_smoke.py
# Run a specific test function
pytest tests/test_smoke.py::test_imports
￿ See: T esting Fundamentals for ML Projects
40
CI/CD and Automation

Y AML Syntax Basics (cont.)
Y AML = human-readable data serialization for configuration files
42

Y AML Syntax Basics (cont.)
# Key-value pairs
name :my-mlops-project
version :"3.10"
# Nested structures
project :
settings :
debug :true
# Lists
dependencies :
-numpy
-pandas
43

Y AML Syntax Basics (cont.)
￿ See: Y AML Syntax Basics
44

Y AML Critical Rules (cont.)
Spaces, not tabs!
# WRONG: Tab indentation
steps :
-name :Checkout
# CORRECT: 2-space indentation
steps :
-name :Checkout
Quote version numbers!
45

Y AML Critical Rules (cont.)
# WRONG: Interpreted as 3.1
python-version :3.10
# CORRECT: String "3.10"
python-version :"3.10"
￿ See: Y AML Syntax Basics
46

What is GitHub Actions? (cont.)
GitHub Actions = CI/CD platform built into GitHub
• Automatically runs workflows on push/PR events
• Sets up fresh environments
• Installs dependencies
• Runs tests
• Reports pass/fail status
￿ See: GitHub Actions & CI Basics
47

Basic CI Workflow (cont.)
48

Basic CI Workflow (cont.)
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name :CI
on :
push :
branches : [main ]
pull_request :
branches : [main ]
jobs :
test :
runs-on :ubuntu-latest
steps :
-uses :actions/checkout@v4
-uses :actions/setup-python@v5
with :
python-version :"3.10"
-run :pip install -r requirements.txt
-run :pytest tests/ -v
￿ See: GitHub Actions & CI Basics49

Workflow Components (cont.)
Component Purpose
name Display name in GitHub UI
on Events that trigger the workflow
jobs Groups of steps that run together
runs-on Operating system for the runner
steps Individual commands to execute
uses Pre-built actions from marketplace
run Shell commands to execute
50

Workflow Components (cont.)
￿ See: GitHub Actions & CI Basics
51

GitHub Secrets Management (cont.)
GitHub Secrets = encrypted environment variables for sensitive data
steps :
-name :Authenticate to Google Cloud
uses :google-github-actions/auth@v2
with :
credentials_json :${{ secrets.GCP_SA_KEY }}
Never hardcode secrets in workflow files!
￿ See: GitHub Secrets Management
52

README Documentation (cont.)
README serves as the project’s front door:
# Project Name
![CI](https://github.com/user/repo/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)
## Quick Start {.allowframebreaks}
1.Clone the repository
2.Create virtual environment
3.Install dependencies
4.Run tests
## Reproducibility Principles {.allowframebreaks}
[150-200 words explaining your approach ]
53

README Documentation (cont.)
￿ See: README Documentation & CI Badges
54

CI Status Badges (cont.)
![CI](https://github.com/USERNAME/REPO/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)
• Green check: All steps passed
• Red X: A step failed
• Y ellow dot: Workflow in progress
Badge proves your setup works in a clean environment!
￿ See: README Documentation & CI Badges
55
Pipelines and Artifacts

What are Pipelines and Artifacts? (cont.)
Data Pipeline: Sequence of processing steps that transforms raw data
Artifact: Any file produced during ML workflow that needs to be saved
• T rained model files ( .pkl ,.h5 ,.pt )
• Preprocessor objects (scalers, encoders)
• Feature definitions and schemas
• Evaluation metrics and reports
￿ See: Data Pipelines & Model Artifacts
57

Pipeline Architecture (cont.)
Each stage produces artifacts that feed into subsequent stages.
￿ See: Data Pipelines & Model Artifacts
58

Artifact T ypes and Storage (cont.)
Artifact T ype Format Storage
T rained models .pkl ,.h5 Cloud storage
Preprocessors .pkl ,.joblib Alongside model
Feature data .parquet Data lake
Metrics .json ,.yaml Git (small)
Configs .yaml ,.json Git
59

Artifact T ypes and Storage (cont.)
Don’t commit large artifacts to Git!
￿ See: Data Pipelines & Model Artifacts
60
Architectural Considerations

What is an LLM? (cont.)
Large Language Model (LLM): Neural network trained on massive text data
• Generates coherent text by predicting next token
• Follows natural language instructions
• Generalizes to tasks not explicitly trained for
# Traditional ML: One model, one task
spam_classifier.predict( "Free money!" ) # "spam"
# LLM: One model, many tasks via prompting
llm.generate( "Classify as spam: 'Free money!'" )
llm.generate( "Translate to Spanish: 'Hello'" )
￿ See: ML vs LLM System

)
llm.generate( "Translate to Spanish: 'Hello'" )
￿ See: ML vs LLM System Architectures
62

ML vs LLM Comparison (cont.)
Aspect T raditional ML LLM Systems
Model source T rained in-house Pre-trained via API
Data requirements Labeled training data Prompts, few-shot examples
Customization Feature engineering Prompt engineering
Artifacts Model files Prompt templates
Inference cost Predictable Variable (token count)
Latency Milliseconds Seconds
63

ML vs LLM Comparison (cont.)
￿ See: ML vs LLM System Architectures
64

T raditional ML Architecture (cont.)
import joblib
model =joblib.load( "model.pkl" )
defpredict(features: dict) ->float:
returnmodel.predict([features])[ 0]
￿ See: ML vs LLM System Architectures
65

LLM System Architecture (cont.)
response =client.chat.completions.create(
model ="gpt-4",
messages =[{"role":"user","content" : prompt}]
)
￿ See: ML vs LLM System Architectures
66

T echnical Debt in ML Systems (cont.)
T echnical debt = implied cost of future rework from quick solutions
Category Description Prevention
Dependency debt Hidden data dependencies Data contracts
Glue code Boilerplate connecting
componentsPipeline abstractions
Configuration debt Scattered settings Centralized configs
Data testing debt Untested data assumptions Schema validation
67

T echnical Debt in ML Systems (cont.)
￿ See: T echnical Debt in ML Systems
68

Preventing T echnical Debt (cont.)
The practices you establish in Milestone 0 are debt-prevention strategies:
• Pin dependencies → Prevents configuration debt
• Use version control → Enables experiment tracking
• Set up CI → Catches integration problems early
• Document data contracts → Prevents dependency debt
￿ See: T echnical Debt in ML Systems
69
Hands-On Demos

Demo: Dependency Pinning MWE (cont.)
# Clone and navigate
cd src/module1/learning-activities/mwe/dependency-pinning
# Create environment
python-mvenv venv
source venv/bin/activate
# Install pinned dependencies
pip install -rrequirements.txt
# Run demonstration
python main.py
If your teammate gets a DIFFERENT hash, your environments differ!
71

Demo: Expected Output (cont.)
============================================================
DEPENDENCY PINNING - Reproducibility Demonstration
============================================================
Environment Snapshot:
Python: 3.10.x
NumPy: 1.24.3
Hash: <16-char-hash>
￿ Numerical Results (seed=42):
Mean: 0.0044569909
Std: 0.9920438018
Key Insight:
72

Demo: Expected Output (cont.)
This is why we pin dependencies with exact versions (==).
============================================================
73
Lab Exercise

Lab: Environment Setup (cont.)
Time: ~30 minutes
Parts:
1. ML Lifecycle Classification - Map code snippets to lifecycle stages
2. Dependency Pinning - Convert ranges to exact versions
3. Project Organization - Identify files for .gitignore
4. Smoke T ests - Write basic import and operation tests
python src/module1/learning-activities/lab/environment-setup.py
75
Assessments

Milestone 0: Environment & CI Setup (cont.)
Deliverables:
1. GitHub Repository with pinned dependencies and clear structure
2. GitHub Actions Workflow that runs smoke tests
3. Documentation (150-200 words) on reproducibility
Rubric (5 pts):
• (2 pts) Environment reproducibility
• (2 pts) Working CI smoke test with green badge
• (1 pt) Documentation quality
77

Homework Assignments (cont.)
Homework T opic
1.1 ML/AI Lifecycle Visualization
1.2 T echnical Debt Analysis
1.3 ML vs LLM Architecture Comparison
1.4 Ethical T rade-offs Case Study
78
Key T akeaways

Key T akeaways (cont.)
1. The ML lifecycle is circular - Monitoring feeds back into data collection
2. Reproducibility requires discipline - Pin dependencies, use virtual environments, automate
with CI
3. LLMs change the paradigm - From “building models” to “building systems around models”
4. T echnical debt compounds - Invest in infrastructure early to prevent costly rework
80

Next Module Preview (cont.)
Module 2: Web & Serverless Model Serving
How do companies like Stripe serve fraud predictions in under 50ms?
• Deploy ML models as production-ready web services
• Build FastAPI endpoints
• Containerize with Docker
• Deploy to Cloud Run and Cloud Functions
